title: A Math-Heavy Quarto Post
text:
Inline notation works with dollar signs, for example \(f : \R^d \to \R\) and \(\E[X]\).
Display equations are written with double dollar signs. Add an identifier like {#eq-risk} after the equation to number and reference it:
\[
R(\theta) = \E_{(X,Y) \sim \mathcal{D}}
\left[
  \ell\{f_\theta(X), Y\}
\right].
\tag{1}\]
We can refer back to equation 1 in prose.

title: A Math-Heavy Quarto Post
text:
Inline notation works with dollar signs, for example \(f : \R^d \to \R\) and \(\E[X]\).
Display equations are written with double dollar signs. Add an identifier like {#eq-risk} after the equation to number and reference it:
\[
R(\theta) = \E_{(X,Y) \sim \mathcal{D}}
\left[
  \ell\{f_\theta(X), Y\}
\right].
\tag{1}\]
We can refer back to equation 1 in prose.

title: A Math-Heavy Quarto Post
text:
2 Aligned Equations
Use standard LaTeX environments for multi-line derivations:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_\theta R(\theta)
&= \nabla_\theta \E[\ell(f_\theta(X), Y)] \\
&= \E[\nabla_\theta \ell(f_\theta(X), Y)].
\end{aligned}
\tag{2}\]
The interchange in equation 2 needs regularity assumptions, which is a nice place to use a theorem block.

title: A Math-Heavy Quarto Post
text:
3 Definitions and Theorems

Definition 1 (Lipschitz Function) A function \(f : \R^d \to \R\) is \(L\)-Lipschitz if, for all \(x,y \in \R^d\),
\[
|f(x) - f(y)| \le L \norm{x-y}.
\]

Refer to definition 1 anywhere after the block.

Theorem 1 (Contraction Mapping) Let \((M,d)\) be a complete metric space. If \(T : M \to M\) satisfies
\[
d(Tx, Ty) \le c\,d(x,y)
\quad \text{for some } 0 \le c < 1,
\]
then \(T\) has a unique fixed point.

theorem 1 is one of the most useful fixed-point tools.

Proof. Choose any \(x_0 \in M\) and define \(x_{n+1} = T x_n\). Then
\[
d(x_{n+1}, x_n) \le c^n d(x_1, x_0).
\]
The sequence is Cauchy, hence converges to some \(x^\star \in M\). Continuity of \(T\) gives \(T x^\star = x^\star\). Uniqueness follows by applying the contraction inequality to two fixed points.

title: A Math-Heavy Quarto Post
text:
4 Useful Macro Examples
The project-wide MathJax configuration in _quarto.yml defines a few shortcuts:
\[
\inner{x}{y} = x^\top y,
\qquad
\norm{x}_2 = \sqrt{\inner{x}{x}},
\qquad
\theta^\star = \argmin_{\theta \in \R^d} R(\theta).
\tag{3}\]
That keeps your posts readable while still rendering proper LaTeX.

title: A Math-Heavy Quarto Post
text:
5 References
You can cite equations like equation 3, definitions like definition 1, theorems like theorem 1, and sections like section 5.

title: Lagrangian Mechanics: The Principle of Least Action and Noether’s Theorem
text: If you are an undergraduate student majoring in physics, I bet that when you heard about the definition of Lagrangian \(L=T-V\) and the principle of least action for the first time, you felt like a fly rubbing its head off. But no worries, I will derive “Lagrangian and Action” from the first principle in this blog and show you how the symmetry is tightly associated with conservation, namely Noether’s Theorem.

title: Lagrangian Mechanics: The Principle of Least Action and Noether’s Theorem
text: If you are an undergraduate student majoring in physics, I bet that when you heard about the definition of Lagrangian \(L=T-V\) and the principle of least action for the first time, you felt like a fly rubbing its head off. But no worries, I will derive “Lagrangian and Action” from the first principle in this blog and show you how the symmetry is tightly associated with conservation, namely Noether’s Theorem.

title: Lagrangian Mechanics: The Principle of Least Action and Noether’s Theorem
text:
2 The introduction of general coordinates
Newton’s Laws become extremely cumbersome when dealing with systems containing many particles and constraints. We need to write down Newton’s Second Law for every particle:
\[
m_i \ddot{\vec{r_i}}=\vec F_i + \vec N_i
\tag{1}\]
where \(\vec N_i\) represents constraint force acting on the \(i\)-th particle, which means it will not contribute to any work if the particle only moves in the allowed direction. Most of the time, it is hard to identify force \(\vec N_i\) since the constraint could be very complicated, and this is where Lagrangian Mechanics comes into play.
Suppose we have \(N\) particles with \(k\) constraint equations in a 3D space, the degrees of freedom of this system is \(3N-k\). Naturally, we could introduce \(3N-k\) new coordinates \(\{q_1, q_2, ..., q_{3N-k}\}\) to match these degrees of freedom, and these coordinates are called “general coordinates”.
Therefore, the position of the i-th particle could be expressed using the general coordinates that occupied all the spatial freedom of the system:
\[
\vec{r}_i=\vec{r}_i(q_1,q_2,\ldots,q_{3N-k},t)
\tag{2}\]
And its differential form could be written as:
\[
d\vec{r}_i
=
\frac{\partial \vec{r}_i}{\partial q_1}dq_1
+
\frac{\partial \vec{r}_i}{\partial q_2}dq_2
+\cdots+
\frac{\partial \vec{r}_i}{\partial q_{3N-k}}dq_{3N-k}
+
\frac{\partial \vec{r}_i}{\partial t}dt
\tag{3}\]
Divide both sides by \(dt\) we obtain the velocity representation under generalized coordinates:
\[
\vec{v}_i
=
\frac{d\vec{r}_i}{dt}
=
\frac{\partial \vec{r}_i}{\partial q_1}\dot{q}_1
+
\frac{\partial \vec{r}_i}{\partial q_2}\dot{q}_2
+\cdots+
\frac{\partial \vec{r}_i}{\partial q_{3N-k}}\dot{q}_{3N-k}
+
\frac{\partial \vec{r}_i}{\partial t}
\tag{4}\]

title: Lagrangian Mechanics: The Principle of Least Action and Noether’s Theorem
section: 3 Analyze the particle system in a “bundle”
text:
3 Analyze the particle system in a “bundle”
As we stated before, the power of Lagrangian mechanics lies in its holistic view of the system, instead of analyzing the system particle-by-particle in Newton’s mechanics. In this section, I will show you how the Lagrangian analyzes the many-particle system in a “bundle”.
Again, Newton’s Second Law for particle \(i\):
\[
m_i \ddot{\vec{r_i}}=\vec F_i + \vec N_i
\tag{5}\]
If we consider a “virtual displacement” \(\delta \vec{r_i}\) within a timescale \(dt=0\), constraint force \(\vec N_i\) doesn’t contribute to any work: \[
\vec N_i\cdot\delta\vec r_i=0
\tag{6}\]
Substitue \(\vec N_i\) with \(m_i \ddot{\vec{r_i}}-\vec F_i\), we obtain the D’Alembert Principle for a single particle:
::: ## D’Alembert Principle
\[
\left(m_i\ddot{\vec r}_i-\vec F_i\right)
\cdot
\delta\vec r_i=0
\] :::
